Peanut Butter Fulfillment Time, Fulfillment Metrics for Service, let's rock and roll. So number of EFT conversions, abandoned hope as we call it. So this is the number of people who signed up for a longer membership during their front end program. And this is my view, this includes anyone, even if they signed up for a bribe offer. So if someone came in for a green player, or you said, I'm going to give you this program for free if you become a member today, doesn't matter. Until the duration of the initial program that that person came in for, which is the psychological soft commitment that they came in with, I do not consider them an EFT. And that is just because we have seen the drop off rates for those first X weeks be larger, independent of whether they take the green player or bribe offer, or what have you, until they are resold after having been serviced for a certain period of time, at least a week or two, typically the conversion will then stick. And so the number of EFT conversions is going is based on the number of people who signed up for a longer membership during their front end program or at the end of their front end program. So someone signed up for a six week program, anyone signs up during or at the way out day and becomes a member of three, six, 12 months, those are all people who are EFT conversions. The percentage is the number of EFT conversions divided by the net starts. So if we had 10 people who actually weighed in and we had eight people who finished, sorry, 10 people who started eight people who converted into EFT at the end, then our conversion percentage would be 80%, right? Eight out of 10 people stayed on as members after the net starts. EFT exit slash cancels. This is the number of clients on EFT who canceled or cannot be recovered from a decline payment. So if someone's sick, if they move away, if they hurt themselves, if they, if their card is declined and you can't recover it, if they cancel straight up, any reason that someone would bill one month and cannot bill the next month is attrition. So that is the exit or a cancel and that counts. Okay. So don't try and fluff your stats. It doesn't help you in any way. The more accurate your stats, the more you can take your emotions out of making the stats, the more accurate they'll be and the more useful they'll be for your business. Okay. Total EFT slash recurring clients, these are all recurring service EFTs. So anyone who is not in the front end program, who is now on recurring and has gone through the EFT conversion, all of those are your total EFTs, your total recurring clients. Okay. That is your recurring revenue. That is that brick that includes your HTTP if you have a second level of service. So like Salmon Privates and your large group. You can account for them separately. That's totally fine. You know, the more you drill down on this stuff, the more useful it is. But for the context of this discussion, I'm just talking about total recurring members. Net clients is going to be the number of EFT conversions, which is the number of real EFT recurring sales essentially, minus the number of EFT exits. So if you converted eight people from your front end program into EFT this month and you had five people leave because of exits for whatever reason, then your net clients would be three. So you gained only three clients to your recurring revenue base this month and that would be your net clients. Okay. Same thing as reverse. You can be negative net clients. You can lose clients in a period of a month if your exits are higher than your EFT conversions. Attrition slash churn. So this is usually expressed as a percentage. I don't want the attrition slash total click division. It's attrition and or churn. I use both interchangeably. You'll see that fairly common throughout all the trainings we have. It is the number of exits divided by the total EFTs that are recurring. So basically it's C divided by D if we're looking over here. So it's this guy divided by this guy. That's all this is. So if you had 10 exits and you had 100 EFTs, then your attrition and churn percent would be 10%. If you had five exits and 100 paying service clients on EFT, then your attrition would be 5%. Note that you can have positive net clients. You can have positive net clients and you can have attrition. So for some reason that sometimes makes people up. I hope that makes sense. It's fairly straightforward. So if you have people who you sign up 20 and 10 people leave, your attrition is still 10%. Even if your membership went up from 100 to 110 because you signed up 20 and lost 10. So you netted 10 clients positive. You still have a 10% attrition. The 100 that started the month, you have 90 of them who finished the month and are billing again the next month. People get really twisted on this. It's very straightforward. 100 started, 90 finished. You signed up 20 during that month. Cool. Now you're at 110, but your attrition is still 10%. Now lifetime value. This is probably the one that most people get the most tricked up about. There are two ways to calculate lifetime value. It is one of those metrics that essentially is, I don't want to say it's impossible to calculate. I want to say it's impossible to be perfect at. Because until your business closes, you don't really know what the lifetime value is because you don't know what the end of time is. Someone can stay with you for, you sign up one client and he stays with you for 50 years and you're two. You don't know what the lifetime value is because you don't know how long they're going to stay. And so there are two methods to calculate your lifetime value of a customer. For me, I like to, if I'm going to think of a lifetime value, I usually only think about it within the context of one year. That's rule number one that I go by. The more cash constrained you are, the shorter you should make that window that you're going to count for lifetime value so that it's actually a useful metric for you for making business decisions. Okay? So the two methods I'm going to show you, I would use both and then I would see which one is smaller and use that one. Okay? Because you always want to be more conservative with your upside and more aggressive with your downside. So if you overestimate your weaknesses and underestimate your strengths, you'll usually make better predictions. Okay? So the first one is method one, which is monthly average EFT. So that's going to be your price. So that's the EFT price is the first one divided by your three month average turn. I don't like doing one month because it's too small of a snapshot. I want to have a larger data size for my three month average turn. And the same thing with the monthly average EFT, I don't want to say my price is X. Right? I want to say, what am I really charging? Okay? I might say my price is this because I see it all the time, but if you have X amount of clients and you divide it out by however many, how much you made that month in recurring, that's going to give you your actual average EFT. So your actual average EFT, the real number, not your inflated number to make you feel better. And then the three month average turn, not the best month you've ever had, but what you're, how many people you lost over three months divided by three, it's basically your averaging or your turn for three months. So I don't want to get too, too into it. And it's just the percentage. So you're doing this, the number that you have for month one, month two, month three, this percentage and you're averaging it out. So if you had 10% turn, 12% turn and 8% turn, your average is 10. Okay? That's it. It's very, you know, add them all together, divided by the amount of months you're averaging. That's all this. Okay? So monthly average EFT price divided by your three month average turn. You can use longer periods of time. Technically, you could use one month. It's just too small of a snapshot for me to make this useful. But if you average 10% turn, means 10% of your customers are leaving every month and your monthly average EFT is 120, then the average customer pays you $1,200. That is the average lifetime value and that is the more theoretical way of calculating it. Okay? The second way to calculate lifetime value that I've grown increasingly fond of over time is a little bit more manual way. So big picture of the way this works is you're just going to look at the people who've canceled and see how long they stayed and just average it and then multiply that average by your average EFT. Okay? So that's simply put what it is and the example kind of breaks it down. So if I had someone, let's say I had five cancellations over a three month period. And so I like to have, I like to use as a three month period as kind of like a good average of what I'm doing because I don't want to let one good month or one bad month skew my numbers from making good forecasts or predictions. So I'll use my three months, all the ones from three months and then I'll divide all of them up. So to have a bigger sample size. So let's just say I didn't want to grab 40 people and then average them for this example. But let's say that over the last three months I had five cancellations and I had someone who had only paid for two months, somebody who paid for five months, 10 months, two months and four months. Okay? So if you add those all up, it's 23 and you divide that by five because there's five examples so that we get the average and the average is 4.6, which means the average person stays with me for 4.6 months. Not too hot. Right? And you multiply that times the cost per month, which is 120. And that's the average based on the math that we already calculated, which means that the average lifetime value of a customer is $552. Mind you, this is why most gyms benefit from having an expensive front end program and it's because most gyms aren't that good and because they're not that good, the front end program is literally makes them more money than the lifetime value of their customers. And so if you're in a situation where you're like, man, I make more money when I charge it, it should just show you that you're not good at fulfillment. And then that's a big problem for you. So that's why I like having these stats so that we can make inferences and make intelligent decisions to find holes. So that's the last of the fulfillment intelligence metrics around service.
